did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs), specifically a U-Net architecture with a SEResNeXt-50 backbone, to perform semantic segmentation of the dermo-epidermal junction (DEJ) in Line-field confocal optical coherence tomography (LC-OCT) images of actinic keratoses. The AI-derived segmentation metrics were then used to automate the quantification of the PRO score (a histological grading system for malignant potential) in a dermatology context.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is explicitly labeled as an 'ORIGINAL ARTICLE' and presents original experimental results. It details a specific methodology involving the training of Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) on a dataset of 237 LC-OCT images and testing on 76 images to assess the PRO score in actinic keratoses. The paper includes a 'Patients and Methods' section, 'Results' with statistical analysis (e.g., kappa coefficients, p-values), and 'Conclusions' based on the authors' own data, which characterizes it as a primary research study rather than a review.

title: Artificial intelligence-based PRO score assessment in actinic keratoses from LC-OCT imaging using Convolutional Neural Networks
brief_summary: This study developed an artificial intelligence model using Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) to automate the assessment of the histological PRO score, which indicates the malignant potential of actinic keratoses, from Line-field confocal optical coherence tomography (LC-OCT) images. The CNN, specifically a U-Net with a SEResNeXt-50 backbone, was trained on 237 LC-OCT images of actinic keratoses to segment the dermo-epidermal junction and quantify protrusions. The model was tested on 76 images, comparing the AI-computed PRO score against a visual consensus grading by imaging experts. The results showed significant agreement between the AI and experts in 75% of cases, with the highest correlation for PRO II (84.8%) compared to PRO III (69.2%) and PRO I (66.6%). Misinterpretations occurred in 25% of cases, primarily due to shadowing of the dermo-epidermal junction and disruptive features like hair follicles. The findings suggest that CNNs provide a feasible tool for automated, non-invasive PRO score assessment to aid in the follow-up of actinic keratoses.

lesion_types_studied.details: The primary target of the study. The CNN was trained and tested on LC-OCT images of actinic keratoses to assess the PRO score (I-III). The dataset included 534 images from 142 AK lesions of 84 patients, with 56 cases histopathologically confirmed.
Used as a baseline/comparator for segmentation training and PRO score metric comparison. The HS dataset included 3,701 LC-OCT images from 142 individuals (volunteers with skin phototype II or VI).
Mentioned in the introduction as the broader disease category and the potential progression target of AK (transformation into invasive KSC), but not directly studied or segmented in the experiments.

all_task_types.details: Semantic segmentation of the DEJ in LC-OCT images using a U-Net with SEResNeXt-50 backbone. The model predicts pixel-level contours of the DEJ to quantify protrusions and undulation metrics.

Multiclass classification of actinic keratoses into three discrete histological grades (PRO I, PRO II, PRO III) based on AI-derived segmentation metrics (DEJ undulation and protrusion depth) processed through a decision tree classifier. The primary task is the automated assessment of the PRO score.

datasets_used.name: Healthy Skin (HS) Dataset
datasets_used.description: A collection of LC-OCT vertical section images acquired using the deepLive device from healthy volunteers with skin phototype II or VI. Images were obtained from the face and upper arm.

use.training: Used to train segmentation models to segment skin layers.
use.other: Used as a baseline for comparing DEJ undulation and protrusion metrics against AK lesions.

datasets_used.name: Actinic Keratosis (AK) Dataset
datasets_used.description: A collection of LC-OCT images of actinic keratoses recruited at the University Hospital of the Ludwig Maximilian University (LMU), Munich. The dataset includes images from patients with histopathologically confirmed AK.

use.training: The full AK dataset (458 images from 104 lesions/59 patients) was used to fine-tune segmentation models. A subset of 237 LC-OCT AK images (from 67 lesions/41 patients) was used to train the PRO score models (decision tree thresholds).
use.validation: A subset of the training data was used to learn thresholds for the decision tree classifier to maximize accuracy.
use.testing: A test set of 76 images (from 38 lesions/25 patients) was used to evaluate the AI-computed PRO score against visual consensus grading by imaging experts.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To analyze normally distributed numerical data (e.g., comparing average absolute distance between PRO scores).
To analyze non-normally distributed numerical data.
To measure the correlation between the outcome of the segmentation model (DEJ undulation and maximum protrusion depth) and the PRO score.
To assess the agreement between visual grading and predicted PRO score.
Attached to the Cohen Kappa coefficient to estimate uncertainty.
To determine statistical significance, with a threshold set to p < 0.05.

hypothesis_testing.test: Student's t test, Mann-Whitney Wilcoxon test, Spearman’s correlation coefficient, Linearly weighted Cohen Kappa coefficient
hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There is no difference in the measured metrics (e.g., average absolute distance, DEJ undulation index, maximum protrusion depth) between the compared groups (e.g., healthy skin vs. PRO I, PRO I vs. PRO II, etc.), or there is no correlation/agreement between the AI-computed PRO score and the visual consensus grading.
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: There is a statistically significant difference in the measured metrics between the compared groups, or there is a significant correlation/agreement between the AI-computed PRO score and the visual consensus grading.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: Statistical significance was determined using p-values with a threshold of p < 0.05. Significant differences were found in segmentation performance (IoU) and average absolute distance between groups (p < 0.001 or p = 0.021). The agreement between AI and visual grading was found to be statistically significant (weighted kappa κ = 0.60, p = 6 × 10−8), ruling out incidental accordance.

Healthy Skin (HS) Dataset.notes: The model was trained on 3,701 images from 142 individuals. The average absolute distance between the ground truth contour of the DEJ and the prediction was significantly lower than in AK datasets (p < 0.001 compared to AK dataset IoU).

Actinic Keratosis (AK) Dataset (Segmenta.notes: The model was fine-tuned on 458 images from 59 patients. The IoU was significantly lower than on the HS dataset (p < 0.001). The average absolute distance increased from PRO I to PRO II (4.7 ± 4.6 μm vs. 8.0 ± 7.3 μm, p < 0.001) and from PRO II to PRO III (8.0 ± 7.3 μm vs. 12.0 ± 6.8 μm, p = 0.021).

Actinic Keratosis (AK) Dataset (PRO Scor.notes: The test set consisted of 76 images (30 PRO I, 33 PRO II, 13 PRO III). Misinterpretation occurred in 25% of cases. Overestimation occurred in 11/76 (14.5%) cases, primarily falsely overestimating PRO I as PRO II (10/30). Underestimation occurred in 8/76 (10.5%) cases, including 3/13 PRO III cases falsely detected as PRO I. The learned thresholds were τ1 = 1.09, τ2 = 1.3, and τd = 64.

architecture_details.task: 2D semantic segmentation of the dermo-epidermal junction (DEJ)

segmentation_training.dataset: Healthy Skin (HS) dataset (3,701 images) and Actinic Keratosis (AK) dataset (458 images)
segmentation_training.method: Trained on HS dataset and fine-tuned on AK dataset

pro_score_model.input_features: DEJ undulation index (σ) and maximum protrusion depth (d)

pro_score_classification.logic: PRO I: σ < τ1; PRO II: τ1 ≤ σ < τ2 and d ≤ τd; PRO III: τ2 ≤ σ and d > τd

preprocessing_techniques: Manual annotation of ground truth contours for the dermo-epidermal junction (DEJ) by an experienced operator
Visual consensus grading of PRO score by two dermatologists, one noninvasive imaging expert, and one resident
Selection of representative LC-OCT images based on visibility of the DEJ and protrusions
Selection of multiple distinct images (up to 4 per lesion) for test set cases exhibiting significant variation of the epidermis and DEJ
conclusions: Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) are helpful for automated PRO score quantification in LC-OCT images.
The AI-automated grading agreed with expert visual grading in 75% of cases (57/76), with a statistically significant weighted kappa of 0.60.
The AI model showed the highest accuracy for PRO II (84.8%) compared to PRO III (69.2%) and PRO I (66.6%).
Misinterpretation occurred in 25% of cases, primarily due to shadowing of the dermo-epidermal junction (DEJ) and disruptive features such as hair follicles.
The findings suggest that CNNs may provide clinicians with a feasible tool for PRO score assessment in the follow-up of actinic keratoses.
A larger training database with diverse skin types and AK lesions is estimated to further improve the AI algorithm's correct DEJ detection and overall performance.
important_remarks: The study is limited by a small test set (76 images from 38 lesions/25 patients) and a training subset for PRO score thresholds (237 images from 67 lesions/41 patients), which may affect generalizability.
The dataset exhibits population bias, as the healthy skin cohort included volunteers with only skin phototypes II and VI, and the AK cohort was recruited from a single center (LMU Munich).
The AI model misinterpreted 25% of cases, primarily due to shadowing of the dermo-epidermal junction (DEJ) and disruptive features such as hair follicles.
Performance varied significantly by PRO score class, with the highest accuracy for PRO II (84.8%) compared to PRO I (66.7%) and PRO III (69.2%).
The study acknowledges that a larger training database with diverse skin types and AK lesions is needed to further improve the AI algorithm's correct DEJ detection and overall performance.
The PRO score thresholds were learned on validation data to maximize accuracy, which may introduce optimism bias in the reported performance metrics.
The AI model overestimated protrusions in 14.5% of cases (mostly PRO I falsely graded as PRO II) and underestimated them in 10.5% of cases.
